name: Col

values.name: cons
values.comment: Update a value in a `Col a`, tracking changes.
values.type: Time.Posix -> a -> Col.Col a -> Col.Col a

values.name: history
values.comment: Get all changes on a `Col a`, in order from newest to oldest.

values.name: init
values.comment: Create a new `Col a`.

values.name: toUpdatedAt
values.comment: For a list of `Col` values on a record `a`, calculate when the record was last updated.

values.name: touch
values.comment: Update a `Col a`'s timestamp without adding a record to its history.

values.name: updatedAt
values.comment: Get a `Col a`'s timestamp\`.

values.name: value
values.comment: Get a value from a `Col a`.

name: Table

unions.name: Id
unions.comment: Represents a unique ID for a `Row` in a `Table a`.

unions.name: Index
unions.comment:
Represent an index for a table.

Internally, an index is:

  - A unique identifier (i.e., the index name)
  - A map between a property on an `a` and an `Int`

When you _query_ a table, you query it by its indexes. This doesn't mean that you need to create an index for every column! The columns
that you should create indexes for are the columns that will help you identify a record, or identify a group of records by some column that
is a member of a smaller set compared to other columns.

Consider the following:

    module User exposing (..)

    import Table

    type alias Record =
        { emailAddress : String
        , catchphrase : String
        , role : Role
        }

    type Role
        = Admin
        | Member

If we need to run queries against `User.Record` to find all users that are `Admin` users, it would make sense to create an index on the `.role`
column, because the `Role` type is a member of a set that only has two values - `Admin` or `Member`.

It wouldn't make much sense to create an index on the `.catchphrase` column - since that column is likely going to be unique for each user, anyway - so if we really needed to do a query against
the `.catchphrase` column, we would have to consider every record separately, no matter what.

unions.name: Predicate
unions.comment:
An opaque function that acts as an interface between the `Index a` values within a table's `Config a`,
and the type of a column that has an `Index a`.

See usage at [`where_`](#where_) for more information.

aliases.name: Row
aliases.comment: Represents a row created in a `Table a`.
aliases.type: { id : Table.Id a, value : a, createdAt : Time.Posix }

aliases.name: Spec
aliases.comment:
A `Spec a b` is a record that contains:

  - An `Index a`, which makes lookups faster for a given column
  - A `Predicate a b`, which allows searching for a `Row a` in a `Table a` by some value `b`, which has the same type as the value indexed
aliases.type: { index : Table.Index a, is : Table.Predicate a b }

values.name: define
values.comment:
Define a table's configuration.

This value returns an empty `Config a`, and can be "built" with other functions in this module whose type signature ends in `Config a -> Config a`.

values.name: delete
values.comment: Delete a record from a `Table`.
values.type: Table.Config a -> Time.Posix -> Table.Id a -> Table.Table a -> Table.Table a

values.name: filter
values.comment:
Query values in a table based on a predicate derived from any column value.

This does _not_ use any of a table's indexes. If you need to use `filter`, then consider either:

  - Creating an `Index a` so that you can have a `Predicate a b` to use with `where_`, or
  - Filtering the result set down as much as possible by `where_` first, before applying `filter`

values.name: getById
values.comment: Get a value by its `id`.
values.type: Table.Id a -> Table.Table a -> Maybe.Maybe (Table.Row a)

values.name: idDecoder
values.comment: Decoder for an `Id a`.

values.name: idEncoder
values.comment: Encoder for an `Id a`.

values.name: init
values.comment: Create a new `Table`.

values.name: insert
values.comment: Insert a record into a `Table`.
values.type: Table.Config a -> Time.Posix -> a -> Table.Table a -> ( Table.Row a, Table.Table a )

values.name: insertMany
values.comment: Insert many records into a `Table`.
values.type: Table.Config a -> Time.Posix -> List.List a -> Table.Table a -> ( List.List (Table.Row a), Table.Table a )

values.name: rowDecoder
values.comment: Decoder for a `Row a`.

values.name: rowEncoder
values.comment: Encoder for a `Row a`.

values.name: select
values.comment:
Select rows from a `Table a`.

This will operate on _all_ rows in a given `Table a`, so it may be wise to use `where_` and `filter` to narrow the `Table` to _what you actually want_ before calling `select`.
values.type: (Table.Row a -> b) -> Table.Table a -> List.List b

values.name: toSpec
values.comment: Create an `Index a` and `Predicate a b` by providing a unique name for the index, and a string representation of the indexed value.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> b) -> (b -> String.String) -> Table.Spec a b

values.name: toSpecBool
values.comment: Convenience function to create a `Spec a b` where the `b` is a `Bool`.

values.name: toSpecId
values.comment:
Convenience function to create a `Spec a b` where the `b` is an `Id b` - i.e., where you'd like to index or filter by a column in your table
that is an `Id b` for some `Table b`.
values.type: String.String -> (a -> Table.Id b) -> Table.Spec a (Table.Id b)

values.name: update
values.comment:
Update a record in a `Table`.

The `Table a` type doesn't track "updated at", since it's assumed that if you care about that, you will wrap each column whose "updated at" time is meaningful, in a `Col a`.

`update` doesn't return a tuple of the updated record and the updated table; `insert` only returns a tuple because `insert` is responsible for creating the `Id a` value.

`update` is an expensive operation, because it rebuilds the indexes for a `Table a`; prefer data structures such that "things that change often" are defined as calls to `insert`,
and "things that only change once in awhile" are handled by calls to `update`.
values.type: Table.Config a -> Table.Id a -> Time.Posix -> a -> Table.Table a -> Table.Table a

values.name: where_
values.comment: Query values in a table based on a `Predicate a b`.
values.type: Table.Predicate a b -> b -> Table.Table a -> Table.Table a

values.name: withIndex
values.comment: Add an `Index a` to a `Table a`.
values.type: Table.Index a -> Table.Config a -> Table.Config a
